comments:
Summary:
This paper implements the categorical compositional distributional model of meaning (Coecke et al., 2010) at scale by learning matrices for relational words (verbs, adjectives, adverbs) from the BNC and applying them to noun vectors to construct sentence vectors. The authors provide a corpus-based procedure for building these matrices, show how sentence meanings follow from type-driven linear maps (pregroup grammars), and evaluate the method on an established intransitive-verb disambiguation dataset (Mitchell & Lapata 2008) and on a newly created transitive-verb dataset. Results match the best published multiplicative baselines on the intransitive task and improve on baselines in the transitive setting.

Strengths:
- The paper provides a concrete, scalable instantiation of an elegant theoretical framework (compact-closed categories + distributional semantics), bridging formal and distributional approaches.
- Clear algorithmic description for constructing relational-word matrices from corpus co-occurrence data; practical choices (BNC, TF/IDF-like weighting, 2k basis) are documented.
- Empirical evaluation includes both replication of a standard benchmark and a novel extension to transitive sentences, which is appropriate given the model’s syntactic sensitivity.
- The method naturally accommodates different arities (intransitive/transitive/ditransitive) and composes in a linguistically guided way, avoiding commutativity issues of add/multiply.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Statistical reporting is limited. Although Spearman’s rho is used, the paper lacks confidence intervals or detailed significance testing between competing models (some p-values are referenced but not fully detailed). Error bars or bootstrap tests would strengthen claims.
- The new transitive dataset and its annotation process are described but more detail is needed on inter-annotator agreement and scoring distribution (how consistent were annotators; what upper bound rho is achievable?). The provided URL helps reproducibility, but more analysis of dataset properties would help.
- Not all reasonable baselines appear in the transitive experiment (Kintsch omitted due to parameter tuning issues). It would be useful to either tune or adapt those baselines so comparisons are comprehensive.
- Computational costs and scalability are only briefly discussed. Building and storing high-dimensional verb tensors can be expensive; empirical runtime/memory numbers or complexity discussion would help practitioners.
- The treatment of function words, quantifiers, and logical connectives is left for future work (acknowledged by authors). While acceptable, a brief worked example or outline for a couple of function words would make the approach more convincing for full-sentence semantics.
- The paper uses pregroup grammars and flattens some compositional order (apply verb to all arguments simultaneously), whereas other grammars (e.g., CCG) apply arguments sequentially. The authors note this but do not empirically explore whether that design choice impacts results.

Suggestions:
- Add statistical significance tests (paired bootstrap or Fisher transformation for rho) between models, and report inter-annotator agreement (upper bound) for both datasets.
- Include runtime/memory measurements for matrix construction and sentence composition on the BNC-sized data to help reproducibility and assess scalability.
- If feasible, provide a small ablation study showing sensitivity to number of basis features, weighting scheme (TF-IDF vs P(w|t)/P(w)), and dimensionality reduction (if applied).
- Discuss or experiment with how the approach generalizes to longer/deeper sentences and with more complex syntactic constructions (coordination, subordination), or at least give complexity bounds for composition operations.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid and well-motivated empirical contribution that operationalizes an important theoretical proposal and demonstrates its practical viability. The evaluation is appropriate and shows the expected advantages when syntactic complexity increases. With clearer statistical reporting and more discussion of computational costs and dataset annotation quality, the paper would be stronger and more useful for practitioners. I recommend acceptance / positive endorsement.
